03 Defining the Core Process - YouTube.mp4
All right. So the first step of playbook creation is to define the process that we're going to be documenting. And the good news is you've already completed much of this step. I don't know if you recall from the session on value creation, but during that session, I kind of asked you to think about and make a mental note of what we refer to as your power stages. Power stages, if you recall, are the truly critical high-leverage actions and activities that define a given value engine. In other words, these are the handful of steps in your growth engines and fulfillment engines that you absolutely must get right, which is why these are the steps that you absolutely should. Fine in a business playbook.
This is so important because when you're starting out with process documentation, the tendency is to merely document the things that you do the most frequently. But let's be honest, the things that you do the most frequently aren't necessarily the things that actually make a difference, that actually move the needle. For example, when we started building... out our playbook library, the first thing that we documented was the process for requesting paid time off. Now, look, I love vacation as much as the next guy, and I, you know, want our team members to take a break and get in some, you know, rest and relaxation from time to time. That said... Was this really the most important playbook that we could create? I mean, does having this process documented truly create more customers or a better customer experience?
I don't think so. And while it may make sense to one day document all of the little tasks and procedures, you know, that exist in your business, that's not where you should start. I'm gonna say that again. That is not where you should start. Instead, start by defining only those actions and activities that are truly critical. And the way to know that is to go back to your value engines and ask the question, which stage or stages in this value engine are truly critical? Where are the power stages? And don't be surprised if there are only two or three power stages in any given value engine. The most that I've seen in a single value engine is seven. And that was definitely an exception. All right.
So the great news is you don't have to document each and every step of each and every value engine, and you definitely don't have to document everything that you do as a company. Start with your power stages. Document those first, and if you have time, you can go back and document some of the other less critical but still important stages. At a later date. Once you have identified a power stage to be documented, I highly recommend that you give that process a name. Now, this may seem a little bit cheesy, but names are easier to remember than descriptions. And there's just something powerful about naming something, right? I mean, it signals that it's important and more important. It signals that you truly own that process, that you have dominion over that process, which is the goal after all.
The final piece of the defined step is to clarify who... Will be responsible for documenting and maintaining this playbook. We call this person the playbook owner because they are uniquely responsible for both building the playbook and for ensuring that it stays accurate and up-to-date. And here's the deal. The owner shouldn't be you. Let me say that again because it's really, really important, okay? As the owner of the company, as the CEO, you should not be the person building out all of these playbooks. There should be someone else within the organization, ideally the person who's already performing the task, who can be responsible for the documentation and the ownership responsibilities. So once you know who that person is and you have clearly defined the power stages that need further documentation, you are ready to move on to the next step.
